does: This paper reframes AI safety from traditional alignment concerns toward a systems-level problem of controlling irreversibility in increasingly dense decision environments. It introduces the concept of decision-energy density—the rate-weighted capacity of a system to generate, evaluate, select and execute consequential decisions—as a quantitative framework. The core contribution is identifying three sovereignty boundaries (irreversible decision authority, physical resource mobilization authority, and self-expansion authority) that determine whether AI systems remain tools within human governance or become de facto autonomous control centers. The paper argues that declining deployment friction in AI fundamentally alters the safety landscape compared to previous high-risk technologies constrained by capital intensity and physical bottlenecks.
connects: This work extends classical control theory and systems safety frameworks to AI deployment at scale, building on decades of research into irreversibility, organizational decision-making, and critical infrastructure governance. It responds to recent concerns about capability overhang and fast takeoff scenarios in AI development, while engaging with alignment literature that has primarily focused on preference specification and local output correctness. The paper also connects to work on institutional fragility, path dependence in technological systems, and how efficiency pressures create unintended structural changes in complex organizations. It synthesizes insights from high-reliability organization theory and safety engineering disciplines with emerging concerns about AI's unique low-cost replicability and embedding.
enables: This framework enables design of governance structures and technical safeguards specifically targeting decision-density control rather than output-level constraints alone. It points toward research on monitoring and limiting decision-authority concentration, designing reversible-decision-first architectures, and building institutional circuit-breakers that can interrupt feedback loops before self-expansion thresholds are crossed. The sovereignty boundary concept could guide development of restricted deployment policies that prevent certain decision types from reaching high-density automation. It also suggests new directions for safety evaluation focused on measuring and predicting decision-energy accumulation in organizational contexts, and for designing AI systems with explicit architectural limits on authority transfer and resource mobilization access.
